credit_headline.narrative: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, growing 17.7% year-on-year — the fastest pace on record and a decisive re-acceleration after April's post-year-end dip. The system added ₹31.2L Cr in FY26, the largest annual deployment tracked, with non-food credit (₹213.8L Cr) driving 99% of the expansion. All four main sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — are growing simultaneously for the ninth straight period, signaling synchronized demand across the economy. Lenders face a high-growth, high-competition environment: expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality as the credit cycle sustains this aggressive pace into FY27.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.title: Bank credit at ₹215.2L Cr — new all-time high
sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding reached ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹3.0L Cr period-on-period (₹212.1L Cr → ₹215.2L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
chain: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, up ₹3.0L Cr from April's ₹212.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
The system has added ₹36.5L Cr since January 2025, with no sign of structural slowdown.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: The credit cycle is back on track. Lenders should prepare for sustained high-volume deployment through FY27 — this is a growth environment, not a consolidation phase. Watch for capacity constraints and asset quality pressure as the system scales.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.title: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25, 17.1% in FY26 — 7.1pp step-up
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year growth rate jumped from 10.0% in the year ending March 2025 to 17.1% in the year ending March 2026, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
This is the sharpest year-over-year step-up recorded, signaling a decisive shift in the credit cycle.
Lenders that scaled underwriting and distribution in FY25 are now capturing compounding gains in FY26.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: The credit cycle has turned decisively. Banks and NBFCs that built capacity early are winning share; laggards face permanent disadvantage. Expect FY27 to sustain this pace unless macro headwinds emerge.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — record annual add
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has remained constant at ₹31.2L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
chain: Banks added ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume exceeds any prior annual addition tracked, signaling aggressive system-wide expansion.
Growth at this velocity can mask emerging stress in weaker pockets or over-leveraged segments.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked. Lenders must audit asset quality and capital adequacy headroom — growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 1.6 percentage points (16.0% → 17.7%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 17.1% peak.
chain: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.0% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.5% upper range threshold.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹0.33L Cr period-on-period (₹1.04L Cr → ₹1.37L Cr), the second consecutive large increase and a new all-time high.

Food credit remains a small fraction of the ₹215L Cr system, but the surge signals active procurement financing.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: Food credit is a rounding error in the system, but the two-period surge signals active procurement financing. Lenders in agri-commodity finance should watch for repayment cycles as the season progresses.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.title: All 4 main sectors growing — breadth at maximum
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire nine-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.

psl.narrative: Priority sector lending is undergoing a structural shift: Social Infrastructure exploded to 70.7% YoY, Housing held at 39.0%, and five segments changed status in a single period — the broadest momentum shift in this dataset. The 81.9pp spread between top and bottom performers is the widest on record, driven by retail and infrastructure categories pulling away while Export Credit and Others remain in contraction (though moderating). Banks are concentrating PSL deployment in consumer-facing segments — housing, education, social infrastructure — and mid-tier enterprises, abandoning export-linked and legacy corporate categories. For lenders, PSL compliance now means overweighting infrastructure and retail; for borrowers in Micro/Small and Medium Enterprises, access is improving as those segments shift to active and strengthening status.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.title: PSL Agriculture 13.8% YoY — 3.5pp deceleration from 17.3%
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 13.84% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 3.49pp from 17.3% → 13.8%, breaking a six-period acceleration from 11.0% in Mar 2025.
chain: Agriculture credit grew 13.84% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026 — a 3.49pp deceleration.
This breaks a six-period climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 to 17.3% in Apr 2026, the first meaningful pullback since the uptrend began.
The deceleration suggests the kharif-driven surge may be moderating, though growth remains above the historical median of 11.6%.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending remains healthy at 13.8%, but the sharp deceleration from 17.3% signals the peak may have passed. Banks should monitor whether this is a seasonal correction or the start of a structural slowdown — hold current exposure but pause aggressive expansion until the trend stabilises.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.title: PSL Education Loans 22.6% YoY — stable near upper range
sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL Educational Loans grew 22.59% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth eased 0.68pp from 23.3% → 22.6%, holding in the 22–24% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
chain: Education loans grew 22.59% YoY in May 2026, down 0.68pp from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 22–24% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 22.6% since the breakout from near-zero growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at high growth confirms this is a structural re-rating, not a cyclical spike.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending is locked in at 22–24% growth — a structural opportunity. Banks should continue scaling origination capacity and expect sustained demand through the academic cycle. The 0.68pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.title: PSL Export Credit -9.7% YoY — contraction easing from -12.0%
sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL Export Credit contracted 9.66% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: The contraction narrowed 2.36pp from -12.0% → -9.7%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -8.4% in Mar 2026.
chain: Export credit contracted 9.66% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026 — a 2.36pp narrowing.
This resumes the easing trend from -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -8.8% in Mar 2026, after Apr's reversal to -12.0%.
The contraction remains near the historical median of -13.9%, signaling persistent weakness in trade finance.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export credit is stabilising but not yet recovering — the move from -12.0% to -9.7% is progress, but growth remains negative. Banks should stay cautious on export lending until the contraction turns positive for at least two consecutive periods.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.title: PSL Housing 39.0% YoY — holding near 40% for six periods
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 38.98% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth eased 0.96pp from 39.9% → 39.0%, holding in the 37–40% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
chain: Housing credit grew 38.98% YoY in May 2026, down 0.96pp from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 37–40% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 36.9% since the breakout from negative growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at near-40% growth confirms this is the structural engine of PSL expansion.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing is the anchor of PSL growth at 39.0% — demand is structural, not cyclical. Banks should maximise exposure to this segment and expect sustained deployment through FY27. The 0.96pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.title: PSL Medium Enterprises 17.8% YoY — reversing deceleration trend
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.84% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 0.59pp from 17.2% → 17.8%, the first uptick after a five-period deceleration from 22.6% in Mar 2025.
chain: Medium enterprises credit grew 17.84% YoY in May 2026, up 0.59pp from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a five-period slide from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 17.9%, signaling this segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises showed a 0.59pp uptick to 17.8%, but it is too early to call a trend reversal — growth remains below the historical p25. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 18% before scaling exposure, as the segment has lost share to Micro & Small throughout the deceleration.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.title: PSL MSME 29.4% in FY26 vs 13.4% in FY25 — 15.9pp step-up
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 15.9-point acceleration.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth more than doubled from 13.4% in FY25 to 29.4% in FY26, marking the largest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: MSME credit grew 13.4% in FY25 and 29.4% in FY26 — a 15.9-point step-up, the largest in this dataset.
This full-year acceleration reflects the structural breakout in Micro & Small lending, which surged from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 29.4% in Mar 2026.
The 15.9pp gap confirms MSME is the primary driver of PSL expansion, anchoring FY26 performance.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME's 15.9pp full-year acceleration from FY25 to FY26 is the structural growth story in PSL. Banks should expect this momentum to anchor FY27 performance — scale origination capacity and monitor credit quality closely as volumes compound at near-30% growth.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.title: PSL Micro & Small 23.7% YoY — 4.8pp deceleration from 28.5%
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 23.69% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 4.85pp from 28.5% → 23.7%, the sharpest single-period drop since the breakout from 13.4% in Mar 2025.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 23.69% YoY in May 2026, down 4.85pp from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
This is the sharpest deceleration since the surge from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 24.8% in Jan 2026, though growth remains near the historical median of 28.5%.
The 4.85pp drop suggests the peak growth phase may be moderating, but the structural trend remains intact above 20%.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Micro & Small decelerated sharply from 28.5% to 23.7%, but growth remains robust at 23.7% — well above the pre-breakout 13.4%. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 20% or decelerates further. The structural trend is intact, but the pace is slowing from the 29.4% peak in Mar 2026.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.title: PSL Others -11.2% YoY — contraction easing from -12.5%
sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL Others contracted 11.20% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: The contraction narrowed 1.25pp from -12.5% → -11.2%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -9.6% in Mar 2026.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: PSL Others is stabilising — the move from -12.5% to -11.2% is the weakest contraction on record, above the p75 of -11.8%. Banks should watch whether this turns positive in the next 1–2 periods, signaling a structural recovery in miscellaneous PSL categories.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.title: PSL Renewable Energy 12 straight periods of YoY growth — record streak
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: The streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026, with no interruption since the series began in Jan 2025.
chain: Renewable Energy has grown YoY for 12 consecutive periods, extending from 11 in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026.
This is the longest positive streak in the dataset, with no interruption since the series began.
The unbroken streak confirms Renewable Energy lending is structurally entrenched in positive territory, supported by policy and ESG tailwinds.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Renewable Energy's 12-period positive streak is the longest on record — this is a structurally entrenched opportunity. Banks should expect sustained demand through FY27 and beyond, with policy support and ESG mandates anchoring growth. Scale exposure with confidence.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.title: PSL Renewable Energy 17.5% YoY — accelerating from 15.6%
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy grew 17.46% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 1.82pp from 15.6% → 17.5%, the first uptick after a six-period deceleration from 78.3% in Mar 2025.
chain: Renewable Energy credit grew 17.46% YoY in May 2026, up 1.82pp from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a six-period slide from 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 21.7%, signaling the segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy accelerated from 15.6% to 17.5%, the first uptick after six periods of deceleration. This may signal stabilisation, but growth remains below the p25 of 21.7%. Banks should monitor whether this holds above 17% for two more periods before scaling exposure — the 12-period positive streak is intact, but the pace is still recovering.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.title: PSL Social Infrastructure 70.7% YoY — surging 35.4pp from 35.3%
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 70.71% year-on-year in May 2026, surging from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 35.44pp from 35.3% → 70.7%, the highest reading in the nine-period series and the largest single-period jump on record.
chain: Social Infrastructure credit grew 70.71% YoY in May 2026, up 35.44pp from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the highest reading in the series and the largest single-period jump, after swinging from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026 to 35.3% in Apr 2026.
The 35.44pp surge confirms extreme volatility — the segment has swung from negative to 70.7% in just three periods.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Social Infrastructure surged 35.4pp to 70.7%, the highest on record, but the extreme volatility — from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 70.7% in May 2026 — makes this segment unpredictable. Banks should treat this as a tactical opportunity, not a structural trend, and avoid scaling exposure until the trajectory stabilises for at least three consecutive periods.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.title: PSL Weaker Sections 9.1% YoY — weakest reading on record
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 9.06% year-on-year in May 2026, the weakest reading in the nine-period series.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 1.61pp from 10.7% → 9.1%, extending a six-period slide from 15.3% in Mar 2025 and now below the historical p25 of 10.9%.
chain: Weaker Sections credit grew 9.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.61pp from 10.67% in Apr 2026.
This is the weakest reading in the series, below the historical p25 of 10.9%, after a six-period deceleration from 15.3% in Mar 2025.
The segment is losing share to Micro & Small (23.7%) and Housing (39.0%), which are growing 2.6× and 4.3× faster.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Weaker Sections at 9.1% is the weakest on record, losing share to Micro & Small and Housing. This reflects either demand weakness or strategic reallocation by banks toward higher-growth segments. Banks should assess credit quality in this segment — if demand is structurally weak, policy interventions may be needed to revive lending.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.title: Housing 39.0%, Social Infrastructure 70.7% — PSL spread hits 81.9pp
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Social Infrastructure grew 70.7% YoY, Housing 39.0%, and Micro and Small Enterprises 23.7% — the three fastest-growing PSL segments.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between top and bottom performers widened from 52.4pp to 81.9pp. Social Infrastructure doubled from 35.3% to 70.7%. Export Credit improved to -9.7% (from -12.0%), Others to -11.2% (from -12.5%). Five segments shifted status: Housing, Educational Loans, and Agriculture moved to active; Medium Enterprises and Renewable Energy moved to strengthening.
chain: Social Infrastructure surged to 70.7% YoY — doubling from 35.3% last period — while Housing held at 39.0% and Micro and Small Enterprises decelerated to 23.7% from 28.5%.
The 81.9pp spread is the widest on record, driven by Social Infrastructure's breakout acceleration and persistent double-digit declines in Export Credit and Others.
Five status shifts in one period — three segments moving to active, two to strengthening — signal broad-based momentum building across retail and infrastructure PSL categories.
Export Credit's contraction moderated to -9.7% from -12.0%, but remains structurally weak; Others declined -11.2%, also improving from -12.5%.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: The 81.9pp spread and Social Infrastructure's 70.7% surge confirm PSL is bifurcating into a high-growth retail/infrastructure core and a contracting export/corporate tail. Housing's shift to active at 39.0% and Educational Loans at 22.6% signal sustained momentum in consumer-facing segments. Medium Enterprises and Renewable Energy moving to strengthening status indicate emerging pockets of acceleration. Lenders should overweight Social Infrastructure, Housing, and Education for PSL compliance; Export Credit remains structurally challenged despite moderation. The five simultaneous status shifts mark an inflection — PSL growth is broadening beyond housing into infrastructure and mid-tier enterprise segments.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit hit a record 20.4% YoY in May 2026, reversing April's deceleration and confirming the sector's acceleration phase is intact. Growth has re-broadened — seven of 10 sub-sectors are now strengthening, led by Computer Software at 54.9% and NBFCs at 33.7%. Within Trade, Wholesale has reasserted dominance at 56.4% share and 22.3% growth, while Retail recovered to 11.3% but remains the weaker segment. Lenders should lean into services exposure, prioritising Computer Software, NBFCs, and Wholesale Trade — these are the highest-growth, highest-momentum segments in the credit market.

sibc-agriculture-share.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.36% — mid-range stability
sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.36% in May 2026.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: Share declined 0.09pp from 12.44% last period, settling near the median of its nine-period range (12.3%–12.5%).
chain: Agriculture holds 12.36% of total credit, down 0.09pp from 12.44% last period.
The share has oscillated within a tight 12.3%–12.5% band for nine consecutive periods, with no sustained directional trend.
This stability indicates agriculture is growing in line with the overall credit pool, neither gaining nor losing structural ground.
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Agriculture maintains a stable 12.4% footprint — proportional growth signals balanced portfolio expansion. Lenders can sustain current allocation without rebalancing pressure; the sector is neither a share gainer nor a laggard.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.title: Agriculture credit at 14.91% YoY — re-accelerating after Apr dip
sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.70% last period.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 1.21pp from 13.70% to 14.91%, reversing the prior period's 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%. The trajectory shows: 13.7% → 14.9%.
chain: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up 1.21pp from 13.70% last period.
The prior period saw a 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%; this period reverses that move, signaling the Mar peak was not a one-time spike.
Growth is now above the upper range (p75 = 14.3%), confirming sustained momentum rather than a cooling trend.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending has regained momentum at 14.91% YoY after a brief Apr slowdown — the re-acceleration confirms the sector's strength is structural, not transient. Lenders should maintain or expand exposure; the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62% is within reach if the next period sustains this pace.

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.title: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25, 15.0% in FY26 — 6.8pp step-up
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.observation: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, marking a 6.8-point acceleration at the Mar 2026 year-end.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year acceleration of 6.8pp reflects a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from FY25 to FY26, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, a 6.8pp acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end.
This near-doubling of annual growth confirms a capex-driven recovery has taken hold, breaking the sector's multi-year slowdown.
The acceleration is the largest in this dataset, signaling a structural shift in industrial credit demand.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry credit has broken out of its multi-year slowdown — the 6.8pp acceleration confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, balance sheet repair, and PLI-linked demand. This is the most compelling industrial lending opportunity in years.

sibc-industry-share.title: Industry share at 21.63% — edging up from nine-period low
sibc-industry-share.observation: Industry accounts for 21.63% of total bank credit in May 2026.
sibc-industry-share.direction: Share rose 0.13pp from 21.50% last period, moving off the nine-period low but remaining near the median of its range (21.5%–21.9%).
chain: Industry holds 21.63% of total credit, up 0.13pp from 21.50% last period, which was the lowest level in this nine-period window.
Despite strong YoY growth (17.55%), the sector is not gaining share aggressively — it is recovering but not reshaping the credit mix.
The modest uptick suggests industry is growing slightly faster than the pool, but not enough to displace retail or services as dominant drivers.
sibc-industry-share.inference: Industry is in a strong growth phase but not yet gaining share structurally — it is recovering, not reshaping the credit mix. Lenders can lean into industrial exposure for growth, but the sector is not displacing retail or services as the dominant driver. Watch for sustained share gains above 22% as a signal of structural rebalancing.

sibc-industry-yoy.title: Industry credit at 17.55% YoY — new record, accelerating sharply
sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, the fastest rate in this nine-period window.
sibc-industry-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 2.49pp from 15.06% last period, breaking the prior maximum of 15.62% and reaching a new high. The trajectory shows: 15.1% → 17.5%.
chain: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, up 2.49pp from 15.06% last period and surpassing the prior peak of 15.62%.
The acceleration is the sharpest single-period jump in this window, confirming the capex-driven recovery is intensifying, not plateauing.
Growth is now at or near the maximum in this dataset, signaling peak momentum in industrial lending.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry lending has broken into new territory at 17.55% YoY — the sharpest acceleration in this window confirms the capex-driven recovery is intensifying. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, infrastructure exposure, and balance sheet repair. Watch for any deceleration below 15% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.title: HFCs at 17.7%, PFIs at 17.0% — near-parity within NBFC credit
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.observation: Within NBFC lending, Housing Finance Companies hold 17.7% share and Public Financial Institutions 17.0%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.direction: The spread between HFCs and PFIs narrowed from 1.0pp last period to 0.7pp, with both segments holding active status.
chain: HFCs hold 17.7% of NBFC credit and PFIs 17.0%, a 0.7pp spread — the narrowest gap in this scan.
The spread has compressed from 1.0pp last period, driven by PFIs closing the gap with HFCs.
Both segments are active, but PFIs are gaining ground within the NBFC mix, likely driven by infrastructure or long-tenor project finance.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.inference: PFIs are closing the gap with HFCs within NBFC credit, now at near-parity (17.7% vs 17.0%). HFCs remain the larger segment, but PFIs are gaining ground — watch for a share crossover if PFI growth sustains. Lenders should assess exposure to both segments as the mix rebalances toward infrastructure-linked finance.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.title: PFIs at 71.8% YoY, HFCs at 19.7% — 52pp spread, both strengthening
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions grew 71.8% YoY in May 2026, while Housing Finance Companies grew 19.7%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: PFIs accelerated from 57.1% to 71.8%, widening the spread from 40.4pp to 52.0pp — the largest gap in this scan. Both segments shifted to strengthening status.
chain: PFIs grew 71.8% YoY in May 2026, up from 57.1% last period, while HFCs grew 19.7%, up from 16.7%.
The 52.0pp spread is the widest in this scan, driven by PFIs sustaining explosive triple-digit-style growth while HFCs hold steady in high-teens.
Both segments are strengthening, but PFIs are pulling away — likely driven by government-backed infrastructure lending or policy-driven credit expansion.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: PFIs are sustaining explosive growth at 71.8% YoY — likely driven by government-backed infrastructure lending or policy-driven credit expansion. HFCs are stable at 19.7% but lagging far behind. Lenders should assess PFI asset quality and concentration risk given the sustained high-velocity expansion; the 52pp spread signals a structural divergence within NBFC credit.

sibc-personal-loans-share.title: Personal loans at 32.81% — slipping from Apr peak
sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 32.81% of total bank credit in May 2026.
sibc-personal-loans-share.direction: Share declined 0.16pp from 32.98% last period, slipping from above the 75th percentile (32.9%) back toward the median of its range (32.7%–32.9%).
chain: Personal loans hold 32.81% of total credit, down 0.16pp from 32.98% last period, which was near the top of the nine-period range.
The prior period saw a 0.38pp jump from 32.59% to 32.98%; this period reverses half that move, suggesting the Apr bounce was temporary.
The share is now near the median, indicating personal loans are growing in line with the pool, not gaining structural ground.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans have given back half of last period's share gain, settling at 32.81% — the Apr bounce was temporary, not a sustained shift back to retail-heavy portfolios. The sector is growing in line with the pool, not reshaping the mix. Lenders should maintain current exposure but avoid over-indexing to retail; the rebalancing toward productive sectors remains intact.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.title: Personal loans at 15.38% YoY — decelerating from Apr high
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down from 16.01% last period.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 0.64pp from 16.01% to 15.38%, slipping from near the upper range (p75 = 15.7%) toward the median. The trajectory shows: 16.0% → 15.4%.
chain: Personal loans grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down 0.64pp from 16.01% last period.
The prior period was near the highest rate in this window; this period marks a pullback toward the median (p25 = 11.8%, p75 = 15.7%).
The deceleration suggests the sector may be plateauing after a strong run, with growth stabilizing in the mid-teens.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Personal loans remain a growth engine at 15.38% YoY, but the deceleration from 16.01% signals the sector may be plateauing after a strong run. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure — especially credit cards and personal loans — as growth stabilizes at elevated levels. Watch for further softening below 14% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.title: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25, 19.0% in FY26 — 7.0pp step-up
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration at the Mar 2026 year-end.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year acceleration of 7.0pp is the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset, reflecting a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from FY25 to FY26.
chain: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, a 7.0pp acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end.
This near-doubling of annual growth confirms a structural shift in services credit demand, not a temporary spike.
The 7.0pp acceleration is the largest in this dataset, signaling services is in its strongest cycle in recent years.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit is in its strongest cycle in recent years — the 7.0pp acceleration confirms a structural shift, not a temporary spike. Lenders should lean into services exposure, particularly NBFCs and trade, while monitoring asset quality as the sector scales rapidly. This is the most compelling services lending opportunity in this dataset.

sibc-services-rotation.title: NBFCs +3.43pp share in 12m — Other Services -2.13pp, rotation mass 3.72pp
sibc-services-rotation.observation: Within services credit, Non-Banking Financial Companies gained 3.43pp share over 12 months, while Other Services lost 2.13pp.
sibc-services-rotation.direction: NBFCs are the dominant gainer, capturing 3.43pp of the services mix. Other Services, Trade, and Transport Operators are the largest losers, shedding 2.13pp, 0.60pp, and 0.56pp respectively. The rotation mass is 3.72pp, indicating significant internal rebalancing.
chain: NBFCs gained 3.43pp share of services credit over 12 months, the largest gain in this scan, while Other Services lost 2.13pp, the largest decline.
The rotation mass of 3.72pp indicates nearly 4% of the services mix has shifted between sub-sectors, a significant internal rebalancing.
NBFCs are strengthening, while Trade, Transport Operators, Professional Services, and Other Services are weakening — the mix is concentrating in NBFCs and away from traditional services.
sibc-services-rotation.inference: Services credit is undergoing a structural rotation toward NBFCs, which have captured 3.43pp share in 12 months — the largest gain in this scan. Traditional services (Trade, Transport, Other Services) are losing ground, shedding a combined 3.29pp. Lenders should prioritize NBFC exposure within services, particularly HFCs and PFIs, while monitoring asset quality in the weakening sub-sectors. The 3.72pp rotation mass signals a significant internal rebalancing, not just overall growth.

sibc-services-share.title: Services share at 28.28% — edging up from Apr dip
sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 28.28% of total bank credit in May 2026.

chain: Services hold 28.28% of total credit, up 0.07pp from 28.21% last period, which marked a pullback from the Mar 2026 peak of 28.47%.

sibc-services-share.inference: Services remain a dominant force in the credit mix at 28.28%, holding near the top of their range despite the Apr dip. sibc-services-share-scan.observation: Within services credit, NBFCs hold 34.5% share, Trade 22.5%, and Other Services 20.2%. Shipping, Computer Software, and Aviation are the smallest at 0.2%, 0.9%, and 1.0% respectively.

chain: Trade's share rose from 22.1% to 22.5%, reversing the prior period's decline and shifting from weakening to active status.

NBFCs remain the dominant segment at 34.5%, but Trade's status shift to active signals renewed stability in commerce financing.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: Trade has arrested its share decline and returned to active status — a sign that commerce credit demand has stabilised. NBFCs remain the anchor at 34.5%, but Trade's recovery to 22.5% suggests lenders should reassess Trade exposure: the weakening trend has reversed. Monitor whether Trade sustains this active status or resumes share loss in the next period.

sibc-services-yoy.title: Services credit at 20.4% YoY — highest on record, strengthening
sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 20.4% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest reading in this 9-period dataset.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 18.6% in April 2026 to 20.4% in May 2026, a 1.82-point jump. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6% → 20.4%.
chain: Services credit grew 20.4% year-on-year, the highest rate in this dataset and a 1.82-point acceleration from the prior period's 18.6%.
The prior period's 0.42-point deceleration (19.0% → 18.6%) has reversed — growth has re-accelerated past the March peak.
This confirms the March high was not a ceiling but a pause in a sustained uptrend.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services credit has broken through the March peak and is now at its fastest growth rate on record. The April deceleration was a false signal — momentum has resumed. Lenders should lean into services exposure: the strengthening status and record 20.4% growth confirm this is the sector to prioritise. If growth holds above 20% in the next period, the acceleration phase is entrenched.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.title: Computer Software surges 54.9% — 7 of 10 services sub-sectors strengthening
sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: All 10 services sub-sectors are growing year-on-year. Computer Software leads at 54.9%, followed by Shipping at 41.0% and NBFCs at 33.7%. Seven sub-sectors are strengthening, three are active.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: Status shifts reversed from the prior period: five sub-sectors moved from active to strengthening — Shipping (41.0%), Aviation (25.8%), Tourism (22.0%), Commercial Real Estate (18.7%), and Trade (17.3%). Computer Software accelerated from 52.7% to 54.9%. The spread between top and bottom widened to 47.3 percentage points — Computer Software at 54.9% versus Transport Operators at 7.6%.
chain: Seven of 10 sub-sectors are now strengthening, up from four in the prior period — Shipping, Aviation, Tourism, Commercial Real Estate, and Trade all shifted from active to strengthening.
Computer Software accelerated from 52.7% to 54.9%, extending its lead as the fastest-growing sub-sector.
The breadth of strengthening status across seven sub-sectors confirms the April plateau was temporary — services credit growth is now broad-based and accelerating.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Services credit growth has re-broadened — seven of 10 sub-sectors are strengthening, reversing the prior period's concentration. Computer Software at 54.9% and NBFCs at 33.7% remain the standout performers, but the shift of Shipping, Aviation, Tourism, Commercial Real Estate, and Trade to strengthening status signals momentum is spreading. Lenders should prioritise Computer Software and NBFCs for highest growth, but also expand capacity in the five newly strengthening sub-sectors. Transport Operators at 7.6% remains the weakest — monitor for stress.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.title: Wholesale Trade regains share at 56.4% — Retail weakens to 43.6%
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Trade credit, Wholesale holds 56.4% share and Retail 44.4%, a 12.8 percentage point spread. Wholesale is strengthening while Retail is weakening.

chain: Wholesale Trade's share rose from 55.6% to 56.4%, reversing the prior period's decline and shifting from weakening to strengthening status.
Retail Trade's share fell from 44.4% to 43.6%, reversing its prior-period gain and shifting from strengthening to weakening status.
This flip confirms the prior period's Retail resurgence was temporary — Wholesale has reasserted dominance within the Trade pool.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.inference: Wholesale Trade has reclaimed share and momentum within the Trade pool, reversing the prior period's Retail resurgence. The shift from weakening to strengthening for Wholesale and the opposite move for Retail signals that B2B commerce financing is outpacing consumer-facing Trade. Lenders should prioritise Wholesale exposure — it is both the larger segment (56.4%) and the share gainer. Retail's weakening status and share loss suggest consumer commerce credit is losing steam.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.title: Wholesale Trade accelerates to 22.3% — Retail at 11.3%, both strengthening
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade credit grew 22.3% year-on-year and Retail Trade grew 11.3%, both strengthening. The growth spread between them is 10.9 percentage points.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both sub-sectors shifted from active to strengthening status. Wholesale accelerated from 18.1% to 22.3%, while Retail accelerated from 8.0% to 11.3%. The growth spread widened from 10.1 points to 10.9 points, driven by Wholesale accelerating faster than Retail.
chain: Wholesale Trade grew 22.3%, up from 18.1% in the prior period — a 4.2-point acceleration and a shift from active to strengthening status.
Retail Trade grew 11.3%, up from 8.0% — a 3.3-point acceleration, also shifting from active to strengthening.
Both sub-sectors have reversed the prior period's deceleration, confirming the April cooldown was temporary and Trade credit growth is re-accelerating.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Trade credit growth has re-accelerated across both Wholesale and Retail, reversing the prior period's cooldown. Wholesale at 22.3% is the clear leader, outpacing Retail by 10.9 points and widening its growth advantage. Lenders should prioritise Wholesale Trade underwriting — it is both the faster-growing and larger segment. Retail's 11.3% growth is a recovery from the prior period's 8.0% low, but it remains the weaker sub-sector. If both sustain strengthening status, Trade credit is back in a growth phase.

retail.narrative: Personal loan growth remains strong but is showing early signs of moderation. Vehicle loans, the second-fastest major category, have decelerated for three straight periods from an 18.6% peak to 17.3% — still robust, but the inflection may be plateauing. Gold loans continue to dominate at 105.5%, while Education (14.0%) and Advances against shares (8.3%) have shifted to strengthening, broadening the growth base. The weak spot: Credit Card Outstanding decelerated sharply to 1.3%, stalling the turnaround signaled last period. Six of nine categories are active, the healthiest breadth in this window, but lenders should watch for further vehicle deceleration and reassess credit card strategies given the momentum loss.

sibc-pl-cc-abs.title: Credit card outstanding at ₹2.95L Cr — down ₹3,481 Cr
sibc-pl-cc-abs.observation: Credit card outstanding fell to ₹2.95L Cr in May 2026, down ₹3,481 Cr from ₹2.98L Cr in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-cc-abs.direction: Volume declined ₹2.98L Cr → ₹2.95L Cr, the second consecutive monthly drop after peaking in Apr 2026.

sibc-pl-cc-abs.inference: The Apr 2026 uptick was a false dawn — card lending is contracting again. Lenders should treat this as confirmation that the low-growth regime is entrenched, not transitional. Focus on portfolio quality and yield, not volume recovery.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 7 straight periods — record run
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: The sub-10% streak extended from 6 periods in Apr 2026 to 7 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
chain: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest run on record.
The streak began in Dec 2025 and has now extended through May 2026 without interruption.
This confirms a structural shift away from the double-digit growth regime that prevailed before Dec 2025.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: Seven months of sub-10% growth is not a cycle — it is the new baseline. Card lenders must permanently reset volume expectations and shift strategy toward margin optimisation and risk-adjusted returns. No recovery is visible.

sibc-pl-cc-share.title: Credit card share at 4.20% — lowest in 9 periods
sibc-pl-cc-share.observation: Credit cards now account for 4.20% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window.
sibc-pl-cc-share.direction: Share declined from 4.28% in Apr 2026 to 4.20% in May 2026, continuing the downtrend from 5.0% in Jan 2025.
chain: Credit card share fell to 4.20% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods.

Gold and housing are capturing the incremental credit flows, leaving cards structurally marginalised.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: Cards are no longer a growth driver for retail lenders — they are losing share in a growing pool. This is a structural shift toward secured lending that will persist. Lenders should reallocate capital to gold and housing.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.title: Credit card growth at 1.33% YoY — down 2.5 points from Apr
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.observation: Credit card outstanding grew 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 3.78% in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 3.78% → 1.33%, erasing the prior month's uptick and returning to the Dec 2025–Feb 2026 range of 1.0%–1.7%.
chain: Credit card growth fell to 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down 2.45 points from 3.78% in Apr 2026.
The Apr 2026 uptick to 3.8% has reversed — growth is back near the 1.0%–1.7% range seen in Dec 2025–Feb 2026.
This confirms the Apr 2026 reading was an outlier, not the start of a recovery.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: The Apr 2026 uptick was noise, not signal — card lending remains structurally subdued at 1.3% YoY. Lenders should abandon volume-growth targets and focus on yield optimisation and portfolio quality through H1 FY27.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.title: Consumer Durables negative for 9 straight periods — record contraction
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.observation: Consumer Durables have posted negative YoY growth for 9 consecutive periods, the longest contraction streak on record.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.direction: The negative streak extended from 8 periods in Apr 2026 to 9 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
chain: Consumer Durables have contracted for 9 consecutive periods, the longest negative run in this dataset.
The streak began in Jan 2025 and has persisted through May 2026 without a single positive reading.
This confirms a structural exit by lenders from consumer durable financing.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.inference: Consumer durable financing is in terminal decline — lenders have effectively exited this segment. Expect continued contraction unless consumption rebounds sharply, which is not visible in the data. No capital allocation warranted.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.title: Consumer Durables share at 0.33% — near irrelevance
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.observation: Consumer Durables now represent 0.33% of personal loans, near the median of the 9-period range.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.direction: Share edged up from 0.32% in Apr 2026 to 0.33% in May 2026, but remains down from 0.4% in Jan 2025.
chain: Consumer Durables account for just 0.33% of personal loans in May 2026.
The 0.1-point decline from 0.4% in Jan 2025 reflects sustained underperformance and lender exit.
This segment is shrinking into irrelevance within retail credit.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.inference: Consumer Durables are no longer a meaningful retail lending category — they represent one-third of one percent of the pool. Lenders have effectively exited. No meaningful origination or portfolio growth expected.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.title: Consumer Durables at -2.64% YoY — decline moderating
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.observation: Consumer Durables outstanding contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, an improvement from the -3.38% decline in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.direction: The contraction moderated -3.38% → -2.64%, the smallest decline since Jan 2025's -2.4%.
chain: Consumer Durables contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, the smallest decline since Jan 2025.
The worst of the contraction was Feb 2026's -9.8%; the rate has improved 7.2 points since then.
The moderation suggests the segment is stabilising, though still in decline.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.inference: The worst of the contraction appears to be behind us, but the segment remains in decline at -2.6% YoY. Recovery is not yet visible — lenders should continue to de-prioritise this category until positive growth returns.

sibc-pl-divergence.title: Consumer Durables -18.01pp below personal loans — sole divergent
sibc-pl-divergence.observation: Consumer Durables is the only personal loan category contradicting the overall personal loans YoY trend, with an 18.01-point gap.
sibc-pl-divergence.direction: Consumer Durables is the sole divergent entity, with a gap of 18.01 points below the personal loans aggregate.
chain: Consumer Durables is the only category contradicting the personal loans YoY trend, with an 18.01-point gap.
This reflects the segment's -2.64% YoY contraction versus the broader personal loans pool's positive growth.
The divergence confirms Consumer Durables is in structural decline while the rest of retail credit expands.
sibc-pl-divergence.inference: Consumer Durables is the only retail segment in contraction — it is diverging sharply from the rest of the personal loans pool. Lenders have exited this category while scaling gold, housing, and education. This divergence will persist.

sibc-pl-education-share.title: Education loans at 2.25% share — lowest in 9 periods
sibc-pl-education-share.observation: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window.
sibc-pl-education-share.direction: Share edged up from 2.24% in Apr 2026 to 2.25% in May 2026, but remains down from 2.3% in Jan 2025.
chain: Education loans represent 2.25% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest share in 9 periods.
The 0.1-point decline from 2.3% in Jan 2025 reflects slower growth versus gold and housing.
Education lending is growing but losing ground relative to faster-moving segments.
sibc-pl-education-share.inference: Education lending is growing but losing share in a growing pool — it is not a growth engine. This is a stable, low-volatility portfolio suitable for defensive capital allocation, not volume expansion.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.title: Education loans at 14.03% YoY — re-accelerating after 6-period slide
sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.27% in Apr 2026.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 13.27% → 14.03%, reversing a 6-period deceleration from 15.9% in Jan 2025 to 13.3% in Apr 2026.
chain: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up 0.76 points from 13.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration in 7 periods, reversing a 2.6-point slide from 15.9% in Jan 2025.
The uptick suggests the deceleration has bottomed, though growth remains below the 15%+ rates seen a year ago.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending has stabilised at 14% YoY after a 6-period deceleration. This is a defensive, low-risk segment — expect continued mid-teens growth but no acceleration. Suitable for stable, risk-adjusted returns.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.title: FD-backed advances at 2.21% share — lowest in 9 periods

chain: Gold loans have grown every period for 12 months straight — the longest run on record.
This is the most consistent growth story in retail credit, with no interruption since the streak began.
The unbroken momentum confirms this is a structural shift, not a cyclical bounce.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.inference: Gold lending is the most reliable growth engine in retail credit. The 12-period streak confirms entrenched, structural momentum — lenders should expand gold loan capacity aggressively and treat this as a multi-year portfolio pillar.

Housing demand is stable but insufficient to defend market share against gold and vehicles.

Watch the next period: if growth holds above 17%, the plateau is healthy; if it drops below 16%, the cycle may be turning.

However, Credit Card Outstanding has lost momentum, decelerating sharply to 1.3% — the anticipated turnaround has not materialized. Lenders should prioritize gold, vehicles, and other PL (12.5%) for capital deployment, monitor Education's strengthening trajectory for early-mover advantage, and reassess credit card strategies given the stalled recovery.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is bifurcating into two regimes: MSME segments are decelerating from their peak but still dominating incremental flow, while large corporates are re-accelerating sharply and starting to defend share. Micro & Small grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026 — down from 32.7% in Mar 2026 but still 1.8× the large corporate rate of 14.4%. The MSME vs large corporate spread collapsed from 20.1pp to 11.8pp this period, the sharpest convergence on record, driven by large corporates surging from 10.0% to 14.4%. Within Infrastructure, Ports (82.8% YoY) and Power (23.8%, now strengthening) are the standout growth drivers, while Railways (-37.6%) and Airports (-14.5%) remain in sharp contraction — the 120.4pp spread is the widest in this dataset. Textiles is recovering unevenly: Other Textiles (17.1% YoY, 45.3% share) is strengthening, while Cotton (3.2% YoY, 35.6% share) is the weakest link. Lenders should prioritise MSME exposure while monitoring the large corporate re-acceleration — if the spread drops below 10pp, the incremental opportunity will shift back to large corporates.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.inference: The status reversal confirms steel is back in favour — lenders are re-concentrating exposure after a brief diversification into non-ferrous metals. Iron and Steel's strengthening status and rising share suggest robust demand from construction and infrastructure. Other Metal's weakening status indicates the prior period's momentum in aluminium, copper, and fabricated products has stalled — watch for another period of share loss to confirm the rotation has fully reversed.

The narrowing spread and three strengthening entities point to broad-based momentum across Chemicals — lenders should maintain overweight exposure to Pharma and selectively back Fertiliser if growth holds above 7% for another period.

sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Edible Oils' 41.8% growth and sustained strengthening status confirm it is the clear growth engine within Food Processing — driven by import substitution, rising consumption, and capacity expansion. Lenders should prioritise exposure here. Tea's 1.4% growth and strengthening status mark a decisive inflection, but the low absolute rate suggests the recovery is fragile — watch for another period above 5% to confirm the trend is sustained. Sugar's -1.6% contraction and declining status signal deepening sector stress — weak prices, inventory buildup, or policy uncertainty are driving lender exits. Avoid new commitments until growth stabilises.

sibc-industry-rotation.title: All Engineering +0.91pp leads rotation — Infrastructure -1.73pp weakening
sibc-industry-rotation.observation: All Engineering gained 0.91pp share of industry credit over 12 months, while Infrastructure lost 1.73pp.
sibc-industry-rotation.direction: All Engineering rose from +0.70pp to +0.91pp, shifting from stable to strengthening. Infrastructure worsened from -1.50pp to -1.73pp, maintaining weakening status. Rotation mass (share of the mix that moved) is 2.83pp.
chain: All Engineering gained 0.91pp share, the largest gain among all 19 industry types, and shifted to strengthening status.
Infrastructure lost 1.73pp, the largest loss, and remains in weakening status — its share is declining despite holding 32.5% of industry credit.
Six of 19 segments are strengthening, three weakening, and ten stable — rotation mass of 2.83pp signals moderate reallocation, with Engineering and Petroleum gaining at Infrastructure's expense.
sibc-industry-rotation.inference: All Engineering's +0.91pp gain and strengthening status confirm lenders are rotating toward capex-heavy machinery, electronics, and capital goods — likely on manufacturing policy, PLI incentives, and export demand. Infrastructure's -1.73pp loss and weakening status signal its dominance is eroding despite holding 32.5% share — growth is concentrating in shorter-cycle industrial segments. Vehicles, Vehicle Parts and Transport Equipment shifted to strengthening at +0.23pp, signalling automotive capex is gaining traction. Food Processing's shift to weakening at -0.17pp confirms the prior period's stress in Sugar and Tea is driving share loss.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.title: Infrastructure holds 32.5% — all 19 industry types active
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure accounts for 32.5% of industry credit, Basic Metal and Metal Products 11.3%, and Other Industries 7.6%. Leather and Leather Products holds just 0.3%.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: Infrastructure's share fell from 32.8% → 32.5%. All 19 industry types remain in active status. The spread is 32.2 percentage points.
chain: Infrastructure's share declined 0.3pp to 32.5%, reversing the prior period's 0.3pp gain, but remains the largest segment by far.
All 19 industry types remain active, with no status shifts — the prior period's broad-based stability continues.
The spread is 32.2pp, unchanged from the prior period's 32.5pp — no major structural reallocation is underway.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Infrastructure's 0.3pp share loss confirms the rotation signal — despite holding 32.5% of industry credit, its dominance is eroding as lenders diversify into Engineering, Petroleum, and Metals. The absence of status shifts across all 19 segments signals broad-based but uneven growth — no major structural reallocation is underway, but the rotation data shows Engineering and Petroleum are gaining ground. Leather and Leather Products at 0.3% remains a negligible segment with no strategic relevance.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.title: Petroleum leads at 40.9% YoY — 16 of 19 segments strengthening
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels grew 40.9% year-on-year, Gems and Jewellery 35.5%, and All Engineering 35.1%. Leather and Leather Products grew just 8.8%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: Petroleum accelerated from 37.8% → 40.9%, maintaining strengthening status. Gems and Jewellery accelerated from 26.0% → 35.5%, flipping from active to strengthening. All Engineering accelerated from 30.8% → 35.1%, flipping from active to strengthening. Sixteen of 19 segments are now strengthening, up from ten. The spread is 32.0 percentage points.
chain: Petroleum grew 40.9%, a 3.1pp acceleration from the prior period's 37.8%, maintaining strengthening status and widening its lead as the fastest-growing industry type.
Gems and Jewellery surged from 26.0% to 35.5%, a 9.5pp acceleration, flipping to strengthening status and reversing the prior period's sharp deceleration.
Sixteen of 19 segments are now strengthening, up from ten — the prior period's concern about concentrating growth has reversed, signalling broad-based momentum is back.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Petroleum's 40.9% growth and sustained strengthening status confirm it is the growth leader, driven by energy transition capex, refining capacity expansion, and petrochemical demand — lenders should prioritise exposure. Gems and Jewellery's 35.5% growth and strengthening status mark a sharp reversal from the prior period's 26.0% deceleration — export demand or working capital cycles have re-accelerated. The surge to sixteen strengthening segments from ten signals broad-based momentum is back — growth is no longer concentrating in fewer pockets. All Engineering's 35.1% growth and strengthening status confirm the rotation signal — lenders are backing capex-heavy segments across the board.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.title: Power steady at 57.0% — Telecom stable at 6.5%
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Infrastructure, Power holds 57.0%, Roads 22.6%, and Other Infrastructure 12.2%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) accounts for just 0.5%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: Power's share rose from 56.8% → 57.0%. Telecommunications held steady at 6.5%, shifting from weakening to active status. The spread is 56.6 percentage points.
chain: Power gained 0.2pp to reach 57.0%, maintaining active status and widening its dominance within Infrastructure.
Telecommunications held at 6.5%, shifting from weakening to active status — the prior period's share loss has stabilised.
All seven Infrastructure sub-segments are now active, with no strengthening or weakening entities — the prior period's Telecom stress has eased, but no segment is gaining ground.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Power's 57.0% share and 0.2pp gain confirm sustained investment in renewable energy and thermal capacity — lenders should maintain overweight exposure. Telecom's shift to active status at 6.5% signals the prior period's stress has stabilised — deleveraging or lender exits may have paused, but the segment is not recovering share. Roads at 22.6% remains the second-largest segment but is not gaining ground — growth is concentrating in Power. With all seven entities active, Infrastructure credit is stable but not rotating — no major reallocation is underway within the segment.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.title: Ports surged 82.8% YoY — Railways collapsed -37.6%
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports grew 82.8% year-on-year, Power 23.8%, and Other Infrastructure 1.5%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) contracted -37.6%, Airports -14.5%, and Telecommunications -7.0%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Ports accelerated from 74.0% to 82.8% and remains strengthening. Power shifted from active to strengthening at 23.8%, up from 21.5%. Railways moderated its decline from -44.1% to -37.6% but remains in contraction. The spread is 120.4 percentage points — the widest on record.
chain: Ports grew 82.8% YoY, the fastest in Infrastructure, accelerating from 74.0% last period.
Power shifted to strengthening at 23.8%, signaling broad-based momentum in core infrastructure.
Railways, Airports, and Telecom remain in decline, with Railways at -37.6% — the deepest contraction.
The 120.4-point spread confirms extreme polarisation: growth is concentrating in Ports and Power while transport segments collapse.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Ports is the dominant growth driver — lenders should prioritise exposure to port operators, logistics capex, and trade-linked infrastructure. Power's shift to strengthening at 23.8% confirms the energy transition and capacity expansion are gaining traction. Railways and Airports remain in sharp contraction — avoid new commitments until growth stabilises. The 120.4-point spread is the widest in this dataset — Infrastructure credit is bifurcating into winners (Ports, Power) and losers (transport).

sibc-large-corporate-share.title: Large corporates hold 67.3% share — down 3.8pp in 6 periods
sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates account for 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, up 0.11pp from the prior period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.3% in May 2026, losing 3.7 percentage points over six periods. The 0.11pp uptick this period is noise against the broader structural decline.
chain: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit, down from 71.0% in Jan 2025 — a 3.7pp loss in six periods.
The 0.11pp uptick this period is the first gain in four periods, but it is too small to signal a trend reversal.
MSME segments are capturing incremental credit at the expense of large corporates — the share loss is structural, not cyclical.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporates are losing their grip on industry credit. The 3.7pp share loss in six periods is the sharpest structural shift in this dataset. Lenders overweight to large corporates are missing the growth — incremental credit is flowing to MSME borrowers. The 0.11pp uptick this period is noise, not a reversal.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.title: Large corporates surged 14.4% YoY — highest in 9 periods
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, the strongest reading in this nine-period window.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 10.0% last period to 14.4%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026. The 4.34pp jump is the sharpest single-period acceleration on record.
chain: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY, up from 10.0% last period — a 4.34pp acceleration, the sharpest in this dataset.
The segment has re-accelerated from 5.5% in Jan 2026 to 14.4% in May 2026, signaling a structural recovery.
Despite the 14.4% pace, large corporates are losing share to MSME segments growing above 20% — the growth is healthy but structurally outpaced.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporates are re-accelerating sharply — the 14.4% pace is the highest in nine periods and confirms the segment is back in growth mode. However, the 14.4% pace is insufficient to defend market share against 26.2% Micro & Small growth and 21.2% Medium growth. Lenders here are capturing healthy flow, but the incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in MSME segments.

sibc-msme-medium-share.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — stable for 5 periods
sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.05pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at 9.7% for five consecutive periods, with the 0.05pp dip this period being noise.
chain: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% of industry credit, unchanged from 9.7% in Apr 2026 (the 0.05pp dip is rounding noise).
The segment has held 9.7% share for five straight periods, signaling it is growing in line with the broader industry pool.
The five-period plateau confirms Medium enterprises are capturing steady flow, not outsized growth.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises have carved out a 9.7% share and are holding it. The five-period plateau signals the segment is growing in line with the broader industry pool, not outpacing it. Lenders here are capturing steady flow, not outsized growth — the incremental opportunity is in Micro & Small, not Medium.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.title: Medium enterprises grew 21.2% YoY — re-accelerating
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 19.9% last period to 21.2%, after decelerating from a peak of 22.3% in Jan 2026. The 1.29pp uptick reverses the prior two-period slowdown.
chain: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY, up from 19.9% last period — a 1.29pp re-acceleration.
The segment had decelerated from 22.3% in Jan 2026 to 19.9% in Apr 2026; the 21.2% reading reverses that trend.
The 21.2% pace is far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, confirming Medium enterprises remain a strong outperformer.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are re-accelerating after a two-period slowdown. The 21.2% pace is healthy and far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, confirming the segment remains a strong outperformer. Lenders should watch for sustained acceleration above 21% — if growth holds here, Medium enterprises will resume share gains.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.title: Micro & Small grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth jumped from 8.9% in FY25 to 32.7% in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in FY25 and 32.7% in FY26 — a 23.8pp step-up, the largest in this dataset.
The 23.8pp acceleration confirms Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, not a cyclical uptick.
Lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment in industry credit — the step-up is unprecedented.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This is the defining inflection in industry credit. Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, and lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment. The 23.8pp step-up is unprecedented — banks and NBFCs scaling this segment are capturing outsized share of incremental credit. The FY26 rate of 32.7% is 3.7× the FY25 rate — this is structural, not cyclical.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.title: Micro & Small hold 23.1% share — up 3.1pp in 12 months
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.06pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then eased to 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 14 months. The 0.06pp dip this period is noise against the broader structural gain.
chain: Micro & Small enterprises hold 23.1% of industry credit, down 0.06pp from 23.1% last period (rounding noise).
The segment gained 3.1pp share from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.1% in May 2026 — the sharpest structural shift in this dataset.
The 3.1pp gain is a direct transfer from large corporates, which lost 3.7pp over the same window.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro & Small borrowers are capturing share at the expense of large corporates. The 3.1pp gain in 14 months is a structural reallocation, not noise. Lenders focused on large corporates are being left behind — the incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in smaller borrowers. The 0.06pp dip this period is noise, not a reversal.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.title: Micro & Small grew 26.2% YoY — decelerating but still dominant
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 30.1% last period to 26.2%, after peaking at 32.7% in Mar 2026. The segment has now decelerated for two consecutive periods, losing 6.5pp from the peak.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY, down from 30.1% last period — a 3.91pp deceleration.
The segment has decelerated for two straight periods, losing 6.5pp from the 32.7% peak in Mar 2026.
Despite the slowdown, the 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the large corporate rate of 14.4% — the segment remains the dominant growth driver.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: Micro & Small credit is decelerating but remains the hottest segment in industry credit. The 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the large corporate rate of 14.4%, confirming the segment is capturing the bulk of incremental credit. Lenders should watch for further deceleration — if growth drops below 25%, the segment risks losing share. The two-period slowdown is the first sign of momentum loss after four periods above 30%.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.title: MSME outpacing large corporates by 11.8pp — spread narrowing sharply
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit is growing 11.8 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in May 2026.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread narrowed from 20.1pp last period to 11.8pp, after widening from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026. The 8.3pp collapse this period is the sharpest single-period narrowing on record.
chain: The MSME vs large corporate growth spread narrowed from 20.1pp to 11.8pp — an 8.3pp collapse, the sharpest in this dataset.
Large corporates re-accelerated from 10.0% to 14.4% while MSME decelerated from 30.1% to 26.2%, driving the convergence.
Despite the narrowing, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates is narrowing sharply — large corporates are re-accelerating while MSME decelerates. However, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit. Lenders overweight to large corporates are seeing improved flow, but the incremental opportunity remains in MSME. Watch for further convergence — if the spread drops below 10pp, large corporates will start defending share.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.title: Other Textiles 45.3%, Cotton 35.6% — 2 segments hold 80.9%
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Other Textiles accounts for 45.3% of credit, Cotton Textiles 35.6%, Man-Made 17.3%, and Jute 1.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: Other Textiles shifted from active to strengthening and gained share, while Cotton Textiles shifted from active to weakening. The four sub-segments span a 43.6 percentage point range, with Other and Cotton together holding 80.9% of the pool.
chain: Other Textiles holds 45.3% of Textiles credit and shifted to strengthening, while Cotton Textiles holds 35.6% and shifted to weakening.
The two largest segments — Other and Cotton — together account for 80.9% of the pool.
Man-Made at 17.3% and Jute at 1.7% are too small to drive the sector — any credit shift in Other or Cotton will determine the entire Textiles trajectory.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Textiles credit is concentrated in Other (45.3%) and Cotton (35.6%), which together account for 80.9% of the pool. Other Textiles is gaining share and strengthening, while Cotton is losing share and weakening — lenders should prioritise Other and watch Cotton closely for further deterioration. Man-Made and Jute are too small to matter — the incremental opportunity is in Other Textiles.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.title: Jute surged 20.2% YoY, Other 17.1% — Cotton slowed to 3.2%
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Jute credit grew 20.2% YoY, Other Textiles 17.1%, Man-Made 8.1%, and Cotton Textiles 3.2%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Other Textiles and Man-Made both shifted from active to strengthening this period. Cotton Textiles shifted from strengthening to active, decelerating from 4.8% to 3.2%. The spread across the four segments is 17.0 percentage points.
chain: Jute grew 20.2% YoY, the fastest in Textiles, while Other Textiles grew 17.1% and shifted to strengthening.
Cotton Textiles decelerated from 4.8% to 3.2% and shifted from strengthening to active — the weakest link in the recovery.
The two largest segments — Other (45.3% share) and Cotton (35.6% share) — are diverging: Other is accelerating at 17.1%, Cotton is slowing to 3.2%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Textiles credit is in a broad-based recovery, but momentum is concentrating in Jute and Other. Cotton Textiles — the second-largest segment at 35.6% share — is the weakest link, decelerating to 3.2% and shifting to active status. Lenders should prioritise Other Textiles (17.1% growth, strengthening) and watch Cotton closely — if Cotton drops below 3%, it will drag the entire sector. Jute's 20.2% surge is impressive but irrelevant at 1.7% share.
